- 
+ # Facebook Growth AI - User Guide
+ 
+ ## Introduction
+ 
+ Welcome to the Facebook Growth AI user guide. This document provides detailed instructions on how to use the Facebook Growth AI system to analyze, schedule, and optimize your Facebook content.
+ 
+ ## Getting Started
+ 
+ ### System Requirements
+ 
+ - Python 3.8 or higher
+ - Internet connection
+ - Facebook Developer account with an app configured
+ - Facebook Page access token with appropriate permissions
+ 
+ ### Installation
+ 
+ 1. Clone the repository:
+    ```bash
+    git clone https://github.com/yourusername/facebook-growth-ai.git
+    cd facebook-growth-ai
+    ```
+ 
+ 2. Create a virtual environment:
+    ```bash
+    python -m venv venv
+    source venv/bin/activate  # On Windows: venv\Scripts\activate
+    ```
+ 
+ 3. Install dependencies:
+    ```bash
+    pip install -r requirements.txt
+    ```
+ 
+ 4. Set up your configuration:
+    - Copy `config.example.json` to `config.json`
+    - Edit `config.json` to include your Facebook API credentials
+    - Alternatively, set environment variables (see Configuration section)
+ 
+ 5. Initialize the database:
+    ```bash
+    python -m src.utils.database_init
+    ```
+ 
+ ## Configuration
+ 
+ ### Configuration File
+ 
+ The system uses a configuration file (`config.json`) with the following structure:
+ 
+ ```json
+ {
+   "facebook": {
+     "app_id": "YOUR_APP_ID",
+     "app_secret": "YOUR_APP_SECRET",
+     "token_file": "tokens.json"
+   },
+   "database": {
+     "type": "sqlite",
+     "name": "facebook_growth.db",
+     "host": "",
+     "port": "",
+     "user": "",
+     "password": ""
+   },
+   "api": {
+     "version": "v16.0",
+     "base_url": "https://graph.facebook.com/",
+     "debug": false,
+     "timeout": 30
+   },
+   "analytics": {
+     "topics_count": 5,
+     "min_engagement": 10,
+     "sentiment_analysis": true
+   },
+   "scheduler": {
+     "schedule_file": ".post_schedule.json",
+     "default_status": "draft"
+   },
+   "logging": {
+     "level": "INFO",
+     "file": "facebook_growth.log",
+     "format": "%(asctime)s - %(name)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s"
+   }
+ }
+ ```
+ 
+ ### Environment Variables
+ 
+ You can also configure the system using environment variables. Create a `.env` file in the project root with the following variables:
+ 
+ ```
+ FB_APP_ID=your_app_id
+ FB_APP_SECRET=your_app_secret
+ DB_TYPE=sqlite
+ DB_NAME=facebook_growth.db
+ LOG_LEVEL=INFO
+ ```
+ 
+ ## Authentication
+ 
+ ### Setting Up Facebook Authentication
+ 
+ 1. Create a Facebook Developer account at [developers.facebook.com](https://developers.facebook.com/)
+ 2. Create a new app in the Facebook Developer Dashboard
+ 3. Add the "Facebook Login" product to your app
+ 4. Configure the OAuth redirect URI (e.g., `https://example.com/callback`)
+ 5. Add the required permissions:
+    - `pages_read_engagement`
+    - `pages_manage_posts`
+    - `pages_show_list`
+    - `pages_read_user_content`
+ 
+ ### Authenticating with the System
+ 
+ 1. Run the authentication script:
+    ```bash
+    python -m src.scripts.authenticate
+    ```
+ 
+ 2. Follow the prompts to:
+    - Enter your app credentials (if not in config)
+    - Open the Facebook login URL
+    - Authorize the app
+    - Copy the authorization code
+    - Enter the code in the terminal
+ 
+ 3. The system will exchange the code for an access token and store it securely.
+ 
+ ## Core Features
+ 
+ ### 1. Content Analysis
+ 
+ The Content Analysis feature helps you understand what content performs best on your Facebook page.
+ 
+ #### Analyzing Page Content
+ 
+ ```bash
+ python -m src.scripts.analyze_content --page_id YOUR_PAGE_ID --limit 100
+ ```
+ 
+ This will:
+ - Fetch the most recent 100 posts from your page
+ - Analyze engagement patterns
+ - Extract common topics
+ - Identify content patterns that drive engagement
+ - Generate a report in the `reports` directory
+ 
+ #### Understanding the Analysis Report
+ 
+ The analysis report includes:
+ 
+ 1. **Engagement Summary**:
+    - Average engagement per post
+    - Engagement trends over time
+    - Best and worst performing posts
+ 
+ 2. **Topic Analysis**:
+    - Main topics discussed in your content
+    - Topic performance comparison
+    - Keyword recommendations
+ 
+ 3. **Content Patterns**:
+    - Impact of post length on engagement
+    - Optimal posting times
+    - Effect of media types (photos, videos, links)
+    - Question posts vs. statement posts
+    - Call-to-action effectiveness
+ 
+ 4. **Recommendations**:
+    - Content improvement suggestions
+    - Posting schedule recommendations
+    - Topic and keyword suggestions
+ 
+ ### 2. Post Scheduling
+ 
+ The Post Scheduling feature helps you plan and schedule your Facebook content for optimal engagement.
+ 
+ #### Viewing Your Content Calendar
+ 
+ ```bash
+ python -m src.scripts.view_calendar --start_date 2023-01-01 --end_date 2023-01-31
+ ```
+ 
+ This displays your content calendar for the specified date range, showing all scheduled posts.
+ 
+ #### Scheduling a New Post
+ 
+ ```bash
+ python -m src.scripts.schedule_post --page_id YOUR_PAGE_ID --message "Your post content" --time "2023-01-15T15:30:00" --status draft
+ ```
+ 
+ You can also use the interactive mode:
+ 
+ ```bash
+ python -m src.scripts.schedule_post --interactive
+ ```
+ 
+ This will prompt you for:
+ - Page selection
+ - Post message
+ - Optional link
+ - Scheduling time (with suggestions for optimal times)
+ - Post status (draft or scheduled)
+ 
+ #### Managing Scheduled Posts
+ 
+ To list all scheduled posts:
+ 
+ ```bash
+ python -m src.scripts.list_posts --status draft
+ ```
+ 
+ To update a post:
+ 
+ ```bash
+ python -m src.scripts.update_post --post_id POST_ID --message "Updated message" --time "2023-01-16T10:00:00"
+ ```
+ 
+ To delete a post:
+ 
+ ```bash
+ python -m src.scripts.delete_post --post_id POST_ID
+ ```
+ 
+ ### 3. Content Optimization
+ 
+ The Content Optimization feature suggests improvements to your draft posts based on historical engagement data.
+ 
+ #### Optimizing a Draft Post
+ 
+ ```bash
+ python -m src.scripts.optimize_post --post_id DRAFT_POST_ID
+ ```
+ 
+ This will:
+ - Analyze the draft post
+ - Compare it to your historical content patterns
+ - Suggest improvements for:
+   - Post length
+   - Questions and calls-to-action
+   - Hashtag usage
+   - Emoji inclusion
+   - Posting time
+ 
+ #### Batch Optimization
+ 
+ To optimize all draft posts:
+ 
+ ```bash
+ python -m src.scripts.optimize_all_drafts --page_id YOUR_PAGE_ID
+ ```
+ 
+ ### 4. Page Insights Analysis
+ 
+ The Page Insights Analysis feature provides deeper analytics about your Facebook page performance.
+ 
+ #### Generating a Page Insights Report
+ 
+ ```bash
+ python -m src.scripts.page_insights --page_id YOUR_PAGE_ID --days 30
+ ```
+ 
+ This generates a comprehensive report on your page performance over the specified period, including:
+ - Page growth (followers, likes)
+ - Reach and impressions
+ - Engagement rates
+ - Audience demographics
+ - Best performing content types
+ 
+ ## Advanced Features
+ 
+ ### Similar Pages Analysis
+ 
+ Analyze pages similar to yours to identify content strategies:
+ 
+ ```bash
+ python -m src.scripts.similar_pages --page_id YOUR_PAGE_ID --limit 5
+ ```
+ 
+ ### Content Pattern Mining
+ 
+ Discover deeper patterns in your successful content:
+ 
+ ```bash
+ python -m src.scripts.mine_patterns --page_id YOUR_PAGE_ID --min_posts 50
+ ```
+ 
+ ### Custom Scheduling Rules
+ 
+ Create custom scheduling rules based on your audience behavior:
+ 
+ ```bash
+ python -m src.scripts.create_schedule_rule --day monday --times "10:00,15:00,20:00" --page_id YOUR_PAGE_ID
+ ```
+ 
+ ## Troubleshooting
+ 
+ ### Common Issues
+ 
+ 1. **Authentication Errors**:
+    - Ensure your app credentials are correct
+    - Check that your access token has not expired
+    - Verify you have the required permissions
+ 
+ 2. **Rate Limiting**:
+    - Facebook API has rate limits that may temporarily block requests
+    - The system implements backoff strategies, but you may need to wait
+    - Consider reducing the frequency of API calls
+ 
+ 3. **Database Errors**:
+    - Ensure your database configuration is correct
+    - Check file permissions for SQLite databases
+    - Verify database connection parameters for other database types
+ 
+ ### Logging
+ 
+ The system logs detailed information to help diagnose issues:
+ 
+ - Check the log file specified in your configuration
+ - Increase log verbosity by setting `LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG` in your `.env` file
+ - For immediate debugging, run scripts with the `--verbose` flag
+ 
+ ### Getting Help
+ 
+ If you encounter issues not covered in this guide:
+ 
+ 1. Check the [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/yourusername/facebook-growth-ai/issues) for similar problems
+ 2. Review the [API Documentation](api_documentation.md) for detailed information about the system's components
+ 3. Submit a new issue with detailed information about your problem
+ 
+ ## Best Practices
+ 
+ ### Content Strategy
+ 
+ 1. **Consistency**: Maintain a regular posting schedule
+ 2. **Variety**: Mix different content types (text, images, videos, links)
+ 3. **Engagement**: Ask questions and include calls-to-action
+ 4. **Timing**: Post when your audience is most active
+ 5. **Quality**: Focus on valuable, relevant content for your audience
+ 
+ ### System Usage
+ 
+ 1. **Regular Analysis**: Run content analysis monthly to track trends
+ 2. **Batch Scheduling**: Schedule posts in batches for efficiency
+ 3. **Optimization**: Always optimize draft posts before publishing
+ 4. **Backup**: Regularly export your scheduled posts and insights
+ 5. **Updates**: Keep the system updated for the latest features
+ 
+ ## Glossary
+ 
+ - **Engagement**: The sum of reactions, comments, and shares on a post
+ - **Reach**: The number of unique users who saw your content
+ - **Impressions**: The total number of times your content was displayed
+ - **CTR (Click-Through Rate)**: The percentage of people who clicked on your content
+ - **CTA (Call-to-Action)**: An instruction to the audience to take a specific action
+ - **Draft Post**: A post created but not yet published or scheduled
+ - **Scheduled Post**: A post set to publish automatically at a future time
+ - **Content Pattern**: A recurring element in content that affects engagement
+ - **Topic Extraction**: The process of identifying main subjects in a collection of posts
+ - **Sentiment Analysis**: Determining the emotional tone of content
+ 
+ ## Appendix
+ 
+ ### API Reference
+ 
+ For detailed information about the system's API, see the [API Documentation](api_documentation.md).
+ 
+ ### Command Line Reference
+ 
+ For a complete list of command-line options, run any script with the `--help` flag:
+ 
+ ```bash
+ python -m src.scripts.analyze_content --help
+ ```
+ 
+ ### Configuration Reference
+ 
+ For detailed information about all configuration options, see the [Configuration Guide](configuration_guide.md).
+ 
+ ### Facebook API Permissions
+ 
+ | Permission | Description | Required |
+ |------------|-------------|----------|
+ | `pages_read_engagement` | Access to page engagement metrics | Yes |
+ | `pages_manage_posts` | Ability to create and manage posts | Yes |
+ | `pages_show_list` | Access to the list of pages managed by the user | Yes |
+ | `pages_read_user_content` | Access to content created by users on the page | No |
+ | `ads_management` | Access to ads management functionality | No |
+ | `ads_read` | Access to ads metrics | No |
+ 
+ ### Database Schema
+ 
+ For information about the database schema, see the [Database Documentation](database_documentation.md). 
```

---

```diff
- 
+ # Facebook Growth AI - Database Documentation
+ 
+ ## Overview
+ 
+ This document provides detailed information about the database schema used in the Facebook Growth AI project. The system uses SQLAlchemy as an ORM (Object-Relational Mapping) tool to interact with the database.
+ 
+ ## Database Configuration
+ 
+ The database configuration is managed through the `ConfigManager` class in `src/utils/config_manager.py`. By default, the system uses SQLite, but it can be configured to use other database engines supported by SQLAlchemy, such as PostgreSQL, MySQL, or MariaDB.
+ 
+ ### Default Configuration (SQLite)
+ 
+ ```json
+ {
+   "database": {
+     "type": "sqlite",
+     "name": "facebook_growth.db",
+     "host": "",
+     "port": "",
+     "user": "",
+     "password": ""
+   }
+ }
+ ```
+ 
+ ### PostgreSQL Configuration Example
+ 
+ ```json
+ {
+   "database": {
+     "type": "postgresql",
+     "name": "facebook_growth",
+     "host": "localhost",
+     "port": "5432",
+     "user": "postgres",
+     "password": "your_password"
+   }
+ }
+ ```
+ 
+ ## Database Schema
+ 
+ The database schema consists of several tables that store information about Facebook pages, posts, insights, and scheduled content.
+ 
+ ### Entity Relationship Diagram
+ 
+ ```
+ +----------------+       +----------------+       +----------------+
+ |      Page      |       |      Post      |       | ScheduledPost  |
+ +----------------+       +----------------+       +----------------+
+ | id (PK)        |<----->| id (PK)        |       | id (PK)        |
+ | name           |       | page_id (FK)   |       | page_id (FK)   |
+ | category       |       | message        |       | message        |
+ | fan_count      |       | created_time   |       | scheduled_time |
+ | created_at     |       | updated_time   |       | status         |
+ | updated_at     |       | permalink_url  |       | link           |
+ +----------------+       | type           |       | created_at     |
+         ^                | link           |       | updated_at     |
+         |                | created_at     |       +----------------+
+         |                | updated_at     |               ^
+         |                +----------------+               |
+         |                        ^                        |
+         |                        |                        |
+ +----------------+       +----------------+               |
+ |  PageInsight   |       |  PostInsight   |               |
+ +----------------+       +----------------+               |
+ | id (PK)        |       | id (PK)        |               |
+ | page_id (FK)   |       | post_id (FK)   |               |
+ | metric         |       | metric         |               |
+ | value          |       | value          |               |
+ | period         |       | period         |               |
+ | end_time       |       | end_time       |               |
+ | created_at     |       | created_at     |               |
+ +----------------+       +----------------+               |
+                                                           |
+ +----------------+                                        |
+ | ContentPattern |<--------------------------------------+
+ +----------------+
+ | id (PK)        |
+ | pattern_type   |
+ | pattern_value  |
+ | engagement_impact |
+ | created_at     |
+ +----------------+
+ ```
+ 
+ ## Table Definitions
+ 
+ ### Page
+ 
+ Stores information about Facebook pages.
+ 
+ | Column | Type | Description |
+ |--------|------|-------------|
+ | id | String | Primary key, Facebook page ID |
+ | name | String | Page name |
+ | category | String | Page category |
+ | fan_count | Integer | Number of page fans/followers |
+ | created_at | DateTime | Record creation timestamp |
+ | updated_at | DateTime | Record update timestamp |
+ 
+ ### Post
+ 
+ Stores information about Facebook posts.
+ 
+ | Column | Type | Description |
+ |--------|------|-------------|
+ | id | String | Primary key, Facebook post ID |
+ | page_id | String | Foreign key to Page.id |
+ | message | Text | Post content |
+ | created_time | DateTime | When the post was created on Facebook |
+ | updated_time | DateTime | When the post was last updated on Facebook |
+ | permalink_url | String | URL to the post on Facebook |
+ | type | String | Post type (status, photo, video, link) |
+ | link | String | URL included in the post, if any |
+ | created_at | DateTime | Record creation timestamp |
+ | updated_at | DateTime | Record update timestamp |
+ 
+ ### PostInsight
+ 
+ Stores engagement metrics for individual posts.
+ 
+ | Column | Type | Description |
+ |--------|------|-------------|
+ | id | Integer | Primary key, auto-incrementing |
+ | post_id | String | Foreign key to Post.id |
+ | metric | String | Metric name (e.g., 'post_impressions', 'post_engagements') |
+ | value | Integer | Metric value |
+ | period | String | Period for the metric (e.g., 'lifetime', 'day', 'week') |
+ | end_time | DateTime | End time for the metric period |
+ | created_at | DateTime | Record creation timestamp |
+ 
+ ### PageInsight
+ 
+ Stores metrics for Facebook pages.
+ 
+ | Column | Type | Description |
+ |--------|------|-------------|
+ | id | Integer | Primary key, auto-incrementing |
+ | page_id | String | Foreign key to Page.id |
+ | metric | String | Metric name (e.g., 'page_impressions', 'page_engaged_users') |
+ | value | Integer | Metric value |
+ | period | String | Period for the metric (e.g., 'day', 'week', 'days_28') |
+ | end_time | DateTime | End time for the metric period |
+ | created_at | DateTime | Record creation timestamp |
+ 
+ ### ScheduledPost
+ 
+ Stores information about posts scheduled for future publication.
+ 
+ | Column | Type | Description |
+ |--------|------|-------------|
+ | id | String | Primary key, UUID |
+ | page_id | String | Foreign key to Page.id |
+ | message | Text | Post content |
+ | scheduled_time | DateTime | When the post is scheduled to be published |
+ | status | String | Post status ('draft', 'scheduled', 'published', 'failed') |
+ | link | String | URL to include in the post, if any |
+ | created_at | DateTime | Record creation timestamp |
+ | updated_at | DateTime | Record update timestamp |
+ 
+ ### ContentPattern
+ 
+ Stores identified content patterns that affect engagement.
+ 
+ | Column | Type | Description |
+ |--------|------|-------------|
+ | id | Integer | Primary key, auto-incrementing |
+ | pattern_type | String | Type of pattern (e.g., 'question', 'cta', 'hashtag', 'emoji') |
+ | pattern_value | String | Specific pattern value or regex |
+ | engagement_impact | Float | Impact on engagement (positive or negative percentage) |
+ | created_at | DateTime | Record creation timestamp |
+ 
+ ## Relationships
+ 
+ The database schema includes the following relationships:
+ 
+ 1. **One-to-Many: Page to Post**
+    - A Page can have many Posts
+    - Each Post belongs to one Page
+ 
+ 2. **One-to-Many: Page to PageInsight**
+    - A Page can have many PageInsights
+    - Each PageInsight belongs to one Page
+ 
+ 3. **One-to-Many: Post to PostInsight**
+    - A Post can have many PostInsights
+    - Each PostInsight belongs to one Post
+ 
+ 4. **One-to-Many: Page to ScheduledPost**
+    - A Page can have many ScheduledPosts
+    - Each ScheduledPost belongs to one Page
+ 
+ ## Database Manager
+ 
+ The `DatabaseManager` class in `src/utils/database.py` provides methods for interacting with the database:
+ 
+ | Method | Description |
+ |--------|-------------|
+ | `create_tables()` | Creates all tables defined in the models |
+ | `drop_tables()` | Drops all tables defined in the models |
+ | `get_session()` | Gets a database session |
+ | `close_session(session)` | Closes a database session |
+ | `close_all_sessions()` | Closes all database sessions |
+ 
+ ## Example Queries
+ 
+ ### Retrieving Page Information
+ 
+ ```python
+ from src.utils.database import db_manager, Page
+ 
+ session = db_manager.get_session()
+ try:
+     page = session.query(Page).filter(Page.id == "123456789").first()
+     if page:
+         print(f"Page: {page.name}, Fans: {page.fan_count}")
+ finally:
+     db_manager.close_session(session)
+ ```
+ 
+ ### Retrieving Posts for a Page
+ 
+ ```python
+ from src.utils.database import db_manager, Page, Post
+ 
+ session = db_manager.get_session()
+ try:
+     posts = session.query(Post).filter(Post.page_id == "123456789").order_by(Post.created_time.desc()).limit(10).all()
+     for post in posts:
+         print(f"Post: {post.id}, Message: {post.message[:50]}...")
+ finally:
+     db_manager.close_session(session)
+ ```
+ 
+ ### Retrieving Insights for a Post
+ 
+ ```python
+ from src.utils.database import db_manager, Post, PostInsight
+ 
+ session = db_manager.get_session()
+ try:
+     insights = session.query(PostInsight).filter(PostInsight.post_id == "post_id_here").all()
+     for insight in insights:
+         print(f"Metric: {insight.metric}, Value: {insight.value}")
+ finally:
+     db_manager.close_session(session)
+ ```
+ 
+ ### Retrieving Scheduled Posts
+ 
+ ```python
+ from src.utils.database import db_manager, ScheduledPost
+ import datetime
+ 
+ session = db_manager.get_session()
+ try:
+     today = datetime.datetime.now()
+     next_week = today + datetime.timedelta(days=7)
+     
+     scheduled_posts = session.query(ScheduledPost).filter(
+         ScheduledPost.scheduled_time.between(today, next_week),
+         ScheduledPost.status == "scheduled"
+     ).order_by(ScheduledPost.scheduled_time).all()
+     
+     for post in scheduled_posts:
+         print(f"Post: {post.id}, Scheduled for: {post.scheduled_time}")
+ finally:
+     db_manager.close_session(session)
+ ```
+ 
+ ### Finding Top Performing Posts
+ 
+ ```python
+ from src.utils.database import db_manager, Post, PostInsight
+ from sqlalchemy import func
+ 
+ session = db_manager.get_session()
+ try:
+     # Get posts with the highest engagement
+     engagement_subquery = session.query(
+         PostInsight.post_id,
+         func.sum(PostInsight.value).label('total_engagement')
+     ).filter(
+         PostInsight.metric.in_(['post_reactions_by_type_total', 'post_comments', 'post_shares'])
+     ).group_by(PostInsight.post_id).subquery()
+     
+     top_posts = session.query(
+         Post, engagement_subquery.c.total_engagement
+     ).join(
+         engagement_subquery, Post.id == engagement_subquery.c.post_id
+     ).order_by(
+         engagement_subquery.c.total_engagement.desc()
+     ).limit(10).all()
+     
+     for post, engagement in top_posts:
+         print(f"Post: {post.id}, Engagement: {engagement}, Message: {post.message[:50]}...")
+ finally:
+     db_manager.close_session(session)
+ ```
+ 
+ ## Database Migrations
+ 
+ The system does not currently include a migration system. When the database schema changes, you may need to recreate the database. Future versions may include a migration system using Alembic.
+ 
+ To recreate the database:
+ 
+ ```python
+ from src.utils.database import db_manager
+ 
+ # Drop all tables
+ db_manager.drop_tables()
+ 
+ # Create all tables
+ db_manager.create_tables()
+ ```
+ 
+ ## Performance Considerations
+ 
+ ### Indexing
+ 
+ The database schema includes indexes on frequently queried columns:
+ 
+ - `Post.page_id`
+ - `Post.created_time`
+ - `PostInsight.post_id`
+ - `PostInsight.metric`
+ - `PageInsight.page_id`
+ - `PageInsight.metric`
+ - `ScheduledPost.page_id`
+ - `ScheduledPost.scheduled_time`
+ - `ScheduledPost.status`
+ 
+ ### Query Optimization
+ 
+ For large datasets, consider the following optimizations:
+ 
+ 1. **Pagination**: Use `limit()` and `offset()` to paginate results
+ 2. **Selective Loading**: Use `options(load_only())` to load only needed columns
+ 3. **Eager Loading**: Use `options(joinedload())` to load related objects in a single query
+ 4. **Caching**: Implement caching for frequently accessed data
+ 
+ ## Backup and Recovery
+ 
+ It's recommended to regularly back up your database, especially before making significant changes.
+ 
+ ### SQLite Backup
+ 
+ For SQLite databases, you can create a backup by copying the database file:
+ 
+ ```bash
+ cp facebook_growth.db facebook_growth_backup.db
+ ```
+ 
+ ### PostgreSQL Backup
+ 
+ For PostgreSQL databases, you can use `pg_dump`:
+ 
+ ```bash
+ pg_dump -U username -W -F t facebook_growth > facebook_growth_backup.tar
+ ```
+ 
+ ## Troubleshooting
+ 
+ ### Common Issues
+ 
+ 1. **Connection Errors**:
+    - Check database credentials
+    - Verify the database server is running
+    - Check network connectivity
+ 
+ 2. **Permission Issues**:
+    - Ensure the application has read/write permissions to the database file (SQLite)
+    - Verify database user permissions (PostgreSQL, MySQL)
+ 
+ 3. **Schema Errors**:
+    - If you encounter schema errors after updating the application, you may need to recreate the database
+ 
+ ### Logging
+ 
+ Database operations are logged to help diagnose issues:
+ 
+ - Check the log file specified in your configuration
+ - Increase log verbosity by setting `LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG` in your `.env` file
+ 
+ ## Conclusion
+ 
+ This documentation provides a comprehensive overview of the database schema used in the Facebook Growth AI project. For more information about the API that interacts with this database, see the [API Documentation](api_documentation.md). 
```

```diff
- 
+ # Facebook Growth AI - Configuration Guide
+ 
+ ## Overview
+ 
+ This document provides detailed information about configuring the Facebook Growth AI system. The configuration system is designed to be flexible, allowing you to customize various aspects of the application to suit your needs.
+ 
+ ## Configuration Methods
+ 
+ The system supports two primary methods of configuration:
+ 
+ 1. **Configuration File**: A JSON file containing all configuration settings
+ 2. **Environment Variables**: Environment variables that override settings in the configuration file
+ 
+ ## Configuration File
+ 
+ The default configuration file is `config.json` in the project root directory. You can specify a different file using the `CONFIG_FILE` environment variable.
+ 
+ ### Example Configuration File
+ 
+ ```json
+ {
+   "facebook": {
+     "app_id": "YOUR_APP_ID",
+     "app_secret": "YOUR_APP_SECRET",
+     "token_file": "tokens.json"
+   },
+   "database": {
+     "type": "sqlite",
+     "name": "facebook_growth.db",
+     "host": "",
+     "port": "",
+     "user": "",
+     "password": ""
+   },
+   "api": {
+     "version": "v16.0",
+     "base_url": "https://graph.facebook.com/",
+     "debug": false,
+     "timeout": 30,
+     "retry_count": 3,
+     "retry_delay": 5
+   },
+   "analytics": {
+     "topics_count": 5,
+     "min_engagement": 10,
+     "sentiment_analysis": true,
+     "language": "en",
+     "store_results": true
+   },
+   "scheduler": {
+     "schedule_file": ".post_schedule.json",
+     "default_status": "draft",
+     "time_zone": "UTC",
+     "buffer_minutes": 15,
+     "max_posts_per_day": 5
+   },
+   "logging": {
+     "level": "INFO",
+     "file": "facebook_growth.log",
+     "format": "%(asctime)s - %(name)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s",
+     "max_size": 10485760,
+     "backup_count": 5
+   }
+ }
+ ```
+ 
+ ## Environment Variables
+ 
+ Environment variables can be used to override settings in the configuration file. This is particularly useful for sensitive information like API credentials or when deploying the application in different environments.
+ 
+ You can set environment variables directly in your shell or use a `.env` file in the project root directory.
+ 
+ ### Example .env File
+ 
+ ```
+ FB_APP_ID=your_app_id
+ FB_APP_SECRET=your_app_secret
+ DB_TYPE=sqlite
+ DB_NAME=facebook_growth.db
+ LOG_LEVEL=INFO
+ ```
+ 
+ ### Environment Variable Mapping
+ 
+ The following table shows how environment variables map to configuration settings:
+ 
+ | Environment Variable | Configuration Path | Description |
+ |----------------------|-------------------|-------------|
+ | `FB_APP_ID` | `facebook.app_id` | Facebook App ID |
+ | `FB_APP_SECRET` | `facebook.app_secret` | Facebook App Secret |
+ | `FB_TOKEN_FILE` | `facebook.token_file` | Path to token storage file |
+ | `DB_TYPE` | `database.type` | Database type (sqlite, postgresql, mysql) |
+ | `DB_NAME` | `database.name` | Database name |
+ | `DB_HOST` | `database.host` | Database host |
+ | `DB_PORT` | `database.port` | Database port |
+ | `DB_USER` | `database.user` | Database user |
+ | `DB_PASSWORD` | `database.password` | Database password |
+ | `API_VERSION` | `api.version` | Facebook API version |
+ | `API_BASE_URL` | `api.base_url` | Facebook API base URL |
+ | `API_DEBUG` | `api.debug` | Enable API debug mode |
+ | `API_TIMEOUT` | `api.timeout` | API request timeout in seconds |
+ | `API_RETRY_COUNT` | `api.retry_count` | Number of retry attempts for API requests |
+ | `API_RETRY_DELAY` | `api.retry_delay` | Delay between retry attempts in seconds |
+ | `ANALYTICS_TOPICS_COUNT` | `analytics.topics_count` | Number of topics to extract |
+ | `ANALYTICS_MIN_ENGAGEMENT` | `analytics.min_engagement` | Minimum engagement threshold |
+ | `ANALYTICS_SENTIMENT` | `analytics.sentiment_analysis` | Enable sentiment analysis |
+ | `ANALYTICS_LANGUAGE` | `analytics.language` | Language for text analysis |
+ | `ANALYTICS_STORE_RESULTS` | `analytics.store_results` | Store analysis results in database |
+ | `SCHEDULER_FILE` | `scheduler.schedule_file` | Path to schedule storage file |
+ | `SCHEDULER_DEFAULT_STATUS` | `scheduler.default_status` | Default post status |
+ | `SCHEDULER_TIMEZONE` | `scheduler.time_zone` | Time zone for scheduling |
+ | `SCHEDULER_BUFFER` | `scheduler.buffer_minutes` | Buffer time between posts in minutes |
+ | `SCHEDULER_MAX_POSTS` | `scheduler.max_posts_per_day` | Maximum posts per day |
+ | `LOG_LEVEL` | `logging.level` | Logging level |
+ | `LOG_FILE` | `logging.file` | Path to log file |
+ | `LOG_FORMAT` | `logging.format` | Log message format |
+ | `LOG_MAX_SIZE` | `logging.max_size` | Maximum log file size in bytes |
+ | `LOG_BACKUP_COUNT` | `logging.backup_count` | Number of log file backups to keep |
+ 
+ ## Configuration Sections
+ 
+ ### Facebook Configuration
+ 
+ The `facebook` section contains settings related to the Facebook API authentication:
+ 
+ | Setting | Type | Description | Default |
+ |---------|------|-------------|---------|
+ | `app_id` | String | Facebook App ID | Required |
+ | `app_secret` | String | Facebook App Secret | Required |
+ | `token_file` | String | Path to token storage file | `tokens.json` |
+ 
+ ### Database Configuration
+ 
+ The `database` section contains settings related to the database connection:
+ 
+ | Setting | Type | Description | Default |
+ |---------|------|-------------|---------|
+ | `type` | String | Database type (sqlite, postgresql, mysql) | `sqlite` |
+ | `name` | String | Database name | `facebook_growth.db` |
+ | `host` | String | Database host | Empty |
+ | `port` | String | Database port | Empty |
+ | `user` | String | Database user | Empty |
+ | `password` | String | Database password | Empty |
+ 
+ #### Database URL Construction
+ 
+ The database URL is constructed as follows:
+ 
+ - **SQLite**: `sqlite:///name`
+ - **PostgreSQL**: `postgresql://user:password@host:port/name`
+ - **MySQL**: `mysql://user:password@host:port/name`
+ 
+ ### API Configuration
+ 
+ The `api` section contains settings related to the Facebook Graph API:
+ 
+ | Setting | Type | Description | Default |
+ |---------|------|-------------|---------|
+ | `version` | String | Facebook API version | `v16.0` |
+ | `base_url` | String | Facebook API base URL | `https://graph.facebook.com/` |
+ | `debug` | Boolean | Enable API debug mode | `false` |
+ | `timeout` | Integer | API request timeout in seconds | `30` |
+ | `retry_count` | Integer | Number of retry attempts for API requests | `3` |
+ | `retry_delay` | Integer | Delay between retry attempts in seconds | `5` |
+ 
+ ### Analytics Configuration
+ 
+ The `analytics` section contains settings related to content analysis:
+ 
+ | Setting | Type | Description | Default |
+ |---------|------|-------------|---------|
+ | `topics_count` | Integer | Number of topics to extract | `5` |
+ | `min_engagement` | Integer | Minimum engagement threshold | `10` |
+ | `sentiment_analysis` | Boolean | Enable sentiment analysis | `true` |
+ | `language` | String | Language for text analysis | `en` |
+ | `store_results` | Boolean | Store analysis results in database | `true` |
+ 
+ ### Scheduler Configuration
+ 
+ The `scheduler` section contains settings related to post scheduling:
+ 
+ | Setting | Type | Description | Default |
+ |---------|------|-------------|---------|
+ | `schedule_file` | String | Path to schedule storage file | `.post_schedule.json` |
+ | `default_status` | String | Default post status | `draft` |
+ | `time_zone` | String | Time zone for scheduling | `UTC` |
+ | `buffer_minutes` | Integer | Buffer time between posts in minutes | `15` |
+ | `max_posts_per_day` | Integer | Maximum posts per day | `5` |
+ 
+ ### Logging Configuration
+ 
+ The `logging` section contains settings related to logging:
+ 
+ | Setting | Type | Description | Default |
+ |---------|------|-------------|---------|
+ | `level` | String | Logging level | `INFO` |
+ | `file` | String | Path to log file | `facebook_growth.log` |
+ | `format` | String | Log message format | `%(asctime)s - %(name)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s` |
+ | `max_size` | Integer | Maximum log file size in bytes | `10485760` (10 MB) |
+ | `backup_count` | Integer | Number of log file backups to keep | `5` |
+ 
+ #### Logging Levels
+ 
+ The following logging levels are supported, in order of increasing severity:
+ 
+ - `DEBUG`: Detailed information, typically of interest only when diagnosing problems
+ - `INFO`: Confirmation that things are working as expected
+ - `WARNING`: An indication that something unexpected happened, or may happen in the near future
+ - `ERROR`: Due to a more serious problem, the software has not been able to perform some function
+ - `CRITICAL`: A serious error, indicating that the program itself may be unable to continue running
+ 
+ ## Using the Configuration Manager
+ 
+ The `ConfigManager` class in `src/utils/config_manager.py` provides methods for accessing configuration settings:
+ 
+ ### Getting Configuration Values
+ 
+ ```python
+ from src.utils.config_manager import config_manager
+ 
+ # Get a specific configuration value with a default fallback
+ debug_mode = config_manager.get("api", "debug", False)
+ 
+ # Get an entire section
+ facebook_config = config_manager.get("facebook")
+ 
+ # Get specific settings using convenience methods
+ db_url = config_manager.get_database_url()
+ facebook_credentials = config_manager.get_facebook_credentials()
+ ```
+ 
+ ### Setting Configuration Values
+ 
+ ```python
+ from src.utils.config_manager import config_manager
+ 
+ # Set a specific configuration value
+ config_manager.set("analytics", "topics_count", 10)
+ 
+ # Save changes to the configuration file
+ config_manager.save()
+ ```
+ 
+ ## Configuration Best Practices
+ 
+ ### Security
+ 
+ 1. **Never commit sensitive information** like API credentials to version control
+ 2. Use environment variables or a `.env` file for sensitive information
+ 3. Ensure the `.env` file and `tokens.json` are in your `.gitignore` file
+ 
+ ### Organization
+ 
+ 1. Group related settings in the appropriate section
+ 2. Use descriptive names for custom settings
+ 3. Document any custom settings in your project documentation
+ 
+ ### Deployment
+ 
+ 1. Use environment variables for settings that vary between environments
+ 2. Create separate configuration files for development, testing, and production
+ 3. Validate configuration at startup to catch issues early
+ 
+ ## Troubleshooting
+ 
+ ### Common Issues
+ 
+ 1. **Missing Configuration File**:
+    - Ensure `config.json` exists in the project root
+    - Check if `CONFIG_FILE` environment variable is set correctly
+ 
+ 2. **Invalid JSON**:
+    - Validate your JSON syntax using a JSON validator
+    - Check for missing commas, quotes, or braces
+ 
+ 3. **Permission Issues**:
+    - Ensure the application has read/write permissions to the configuration file
+    - Check permissions for the token file and log file
+ 
+ ### Debugging Configuration
+ 
+ To debug configuration issues, you can print the current configuration:
+ 
+ ```python
+ from src.utils.config_manager import config_manager
+ import json
+ 
+ # Print the entire configuration
+ print(json.dumps(config_manager._config, indent=2))
+ ```
+ 
+ ## Example Configurations
+ 
+ ### Minimal Configuration
+ 
+ ```json
+ {
+   "facebook": {
+     "app_id": "YOUR_APP_ID",
+     "app_secret": "YOUR_APP_SECRET"
+   }
+ }
+ ```
+ 
+ ### Development Configuration
+ 
+ ```json
+ {
+   "facebook": {
+     "app_id": "YOUR_DEV_APP_ID",
+     "app_secret": "YOUR_DEV_APP_SECRET"
+   },
+   "database": {
+     "type": "sqlite",
+     "name": "facebook_growth_dev.db"
+   },
+   "api": {
+     "debug": true
+   },
+   "logging": {
+     "level": "DEBUG"
+   }
+ }
+ ```
+ 
+ ### Production Configuration
+ 
+ ```json
+ {
+   "database": {
+     "type": "postgresql",
+     "name": "facebook_growth",
+     "host": "db.example.com",
+     "port": "5432",
+     "user": "app_user",
+     "password": "use_env_var_instead"
+   },
+   "api": {
+     "timeout": 60,
+     "retry_count": 5
+   },
+   "logging": {
+     "level": "WARNING",
+     "max_size": 104857600,
+     "backup_count": 10
+   }
+ }
+ ```
+ 
+ ## Conclusion
+ 
+ This configuration guide provides a comprehensive overview of the configuration options available in the Facebook Growth AI project. By properly configuring the system, you can customize its behavior to suit your specific needs and environment.
+ 
+ For more information about the API that uses this configuration, see the [API Documentation](api_documentation.md). 
```

---

```diff
- 
+ # Facebook Growth AI - Development Guide
+ 
+ ## Overview
+ 
+ This document provides guidelines and best practices for developers contributing to the Facebook Growth AI project. It covers the development environment setup, coding standards, testing procedures, and contribution workflow.
+ 
+ ## Development Environment Setup
+ 
+ ### Prerequisites
+ 
+ - Python 3.8 or higher
+ - Git
+ - A code editor or IDE (VS Code, PyCharm, etc.)
+ - Facebook Developer account (for API testing)
+ 
+ ### Setting Up the Development Environment
+ 
+ 1. Clone the repository:
+    ```bash
+    git clone https://github.com/yourusername/facebook-growth-ai.git
+    cd facebook-growth-ai
+    ```
+ 
+ 2. Create a virtual environment:
+    ```bash
+    python -m venv venv
+    source venv/bin/activate  # On Windows: venv\Scripts\activate
+    ```
+ 
+ 3. Install development dependencies:
+    ```bash
+    pip install -r requirements-dev.txt
+    ```
+ 
+ 4. Set up pre-commit hooks:
+    ```bash
+    pre-commit install
+    ```
+ 
+ 5. Create a development configuration:
+    - Copy `config.example.json` to `config.dev.json`
+    - Edit `config.dev.json` with your development settings
+    - Create a `.env` file with your sensitive credentials
+ 
+ 6. Initialize the database:
+    ```bash
+    python -m src.utils.database_init
+    ```
+ 
+ ## Project Structure
+ 
+ The project follows a modular structure to organize code logically:
+ 
+ ```
+ facebook-growth-ai/
+ ├── docs/                     # Documentation
+ │   ├── api_documentation.md
+ │   ├── user_guide.md
+ │   ├── database_documentation.md
+ │   └── configuration_guide.md
+ ├── src/                      # Source code
+ │   ├── api/                  # API integration
+ │   │   ├── facebook_api.py
+ │   │   └── auth_manager.py
+ │   ├── analytics/            # Content analysis
+ │   │   └── content_analyzer.py
+ │   ├── scheduler/            # Post scheduling
+ │   │   └── post_scheduler.py
+ │   ├── utils/                # Utilities
+ │   │   ├── config_manager.py
+ │   │   └── database.py
+ │   └── scripts/              # Command-line scripts
+ │       ├── analyze_content.py
+ │       └── schedule_post.py
+ ├── tests/                    # Test suite
+ │   ├── unit/
+ │   ├── integration/
+ │   └── fixtures/
+ ├── .env.example              # Example environment variables
+ ├── config.example.json       # Example configuration
+ ├── requirements.txt          # Production dependencies
+ ├── requirements-dev.txt      # Development dependencies
+ └── README.md                 # Project overview
+ ```
+ 
+ ## Coding Standards
+ 
+ ### Python Style Guide
+ 
+ The project follows the [PEP 8](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/) style guide with the following specifics:
+ 
+ - Use 4 spaces for indentation
+ - Maximum line length of 100 characters
+ - Use docstrings for all public modules, functions, classes, and methods
+ - Use type hints for function parameters and return values
+ 
+ ### Docstring Format
+ 
+ Use Google-style docstrings:
+ 
+ ```python
+ def function_with_types_in_docstring(param1, param2):
+     """Example function with types documented in the docstring.
+     
+     `PEP 484`_ type annotations are supported. If attribute, parameter, and
+     return types are annotated according to `PEP 484`_, they do not need to be
+     included in the docstring.
+     
+     Args:
+         param1 (int): The first parameter.
+         param2 (str): The second parameter.
+     
+     Returns:
+         bool: The return value. True for success, False otherwise.
+         
+     Raises:
+         ValueError: If param1 is negative.
+         TypeError: If param2 is not a string.
+     
+     Example:
+         >>> function_with_types_in_docstring(1, '2')
+         True
+     """
+     if param1 < 0:
+         raise ValueError("param1 must be positive")
+     if not isinstance(param2, str):
+         raise TypeError("param2 must be a string")
+     return True
+ ```
+ 
+ ### Code Formatting
+ 
+ The project uses the following tools for code formatting and linting:
+ 
+ - **Black**: For automatic code formatting
+ - **isort**: For import sorting
+ - **flake8**: For code linting
+ - **mypy**: For static type checking
+ 
+ You can run these tools manually:
+ 
+ ```bash
+ # Format code
+ black src tests
+ 
+ # Sort imports
+ isort src tests
+ 
+ # Lint code
+ flake8 src tests
+ 
+ # Type check
+ mypy src
+ ```
+ 
+ These checks are also run automatically by pre-commit hooks.
+ 
+ ## Testing
+ 
+ ### Test Framework
+ 
+ The project uses pytest for testing. Tests are organized into:
+ 
+ - **Unit tests**: Test individual components in isolation
+ - **Integration tests**: Test interactions between components
+ - **Functional tests**: Test end-to-end functionality
+ 
+ ### Running Tests
+ 
+ To run the test suite:
+ 
+ ```bash
+ # Run all tests
+ pytest
+ 
+ # Run with coverage report
+ pytest --cov=src
+ 
+ # Run specific test file
+ pytest tests/unit/test_facebook_api.py
+ 
+ # Run tests matching a pattern
+ pytest -k "facebook_api"
+ ```
+ 
+ ### Writing Tests
+ 
+ Follow these guidelines when writing tests:
+ 
+ 1. **Test file organization**: Place tests in the appropriate directory (unit, integration, functional)
+ 2. **Naming convention**: Test files should be named `test_*.py`
+ 3. **Test functions**: Test functions should be named `test_*`
+ 4. **Fixtures**: Use pytest fixtures for test setup and teardown
+ 5. **Mocking**: Use unittest.mock or pytest-mock for external dependencies
+ 6. **Assertions**: Use pytest's built-in assertions
+ 
+ Example test:
+ 
+ ```python
+ import pytest
+ from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock
+ from src.api.facebook_api import FacebookAPIClient
+ 
+ @pytest.fixture
+ def facebook_client():
+     """Create a FacebookAPIClient instance for testing."""
+     return FacebookAPIClient(access_token="test_token")
+ 
+ def test_get_page_info(facebook_client):
+     """Test that get_page_info returns the expected data."""
+     # Arrange
+     expected_data = {"id": "123", "name": "Test Page"}
+     
+     # Mock the requests.get method
+     with patch("requests.get") as mock_get:
+         mock_response = MagicMock()
+         mock_response.json.return_value = expected_data
+         mock_response.status_code = 200
+         mock_get.return_value = mock_response
+         
+         # Act
+         result = facebook_client.get_page_info("123")
+         
+         # Assert
+         assert result == expected_data
+         mock_get.assert_called_once()
+         assert "123" in mock_get.call_args[0][0]
+ ```
+ 
+ ### Test Coverage
+ 
+ Aim for at least 80% test coverage for all code. Critical components should have higher coverage.
+ 
+ ## Version Control
+ 
+ ### Branching Strategy
+ 
+ The project uses a simplified Git flow:
+ 
+ - `main`: Production-ready code
+ - `develop`: Integration branch for features
+ - `feature/*`: Feature branches
+ - `bugfix/*`: Bug fix branches
+ - `release/*`: Release preparation branches
+ 
+ ### Commit Messages
+ 
+ Follow the [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) specification:
+ 
+ ```
+ <type>[optional scope]: <description>
+ 
+ [optional body]
+ 
+ [optional footer(s)]
+ ```
+ 
+ Types:
+ - `feat`: A new feature
+ - `fix`: A bug fix
+ - `docs`: Documentation changes
+ - `style`: Changes that do not affect the meaning of the code
+ - `refactor`: Code changes that neither fix a bug nor add a feature
+ - `perf`: Performance improvements
+ - `test`: Adding or correcting tests
+ - `chore`: Changes to the build process or auxiliary tools
+ 
+ Example:
+ ```
+ feat(api): add endpoint for retrieving page insights
+ 
+ - Adds new method get_page_insights to FacebookAPIClient
+ - Includes period and metrics parameters
+ - Updates documentation
+ 
+ Closes #123
+ ```
+ 
+ ## Pull Request Process
+ 
+ 1. Create a feature branch from `develop`
+ 2. Implement your changes with appropriate tests
+ 3. Ensure all tests pass and code meets style guidelines
+ 4. Update documentation as needed
+ 5. Submit a pull request to the `develop` branch
+ 6. Request review from at least one team member
+ 7. Address any feedback from the review
+ 8. Once approved, the PR will be merged
+ 
+ ## Dependency Management
+ 
+ ### Adding Dependencies
+ 
+ To add a new dependency:
+ 
+ 1. Add it to `requirements.txt` (or `requirements-dev.txt` for development dependencies)
+ 2. Document why the dependency is needed in the PR
+ 3. Consider the license and maintenance status of the dependency
+ 
+ ### Updating Dependencies
+ 
+ Regularly update dependencies to get bug fixes and security patches:
+ 
+ ```bash
+ pip install -U -r requirements.txt
+ pip freeze > requirements.txt
+ ```
+ 
+ ## Documentation
+ 
+ ### Code Documentation
+ 
+ - Document all public modules, classes, methods, and functions
+ - Use type hints and docstrings
+ - Keep documentation up-to-date when changing code
+ 
+ ### Project Documentation
+ 
+ Update the following documentation when making significant changes:
+ 
+ - `README.md`: Project overview
+ - `docs/api_documentation.md`: API reference
+ - `docs/user_guide.md`: User instructions
+ - `docs/database_documentation.md`: Database schema
+ - `docs/configuration_guide.md`: Configuration options
+ 
+ ## Logging
+ 
+ Use the built-in logging system for all log messages:
+ 
+ ```python
+ import logging
+ 
+ logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
+ 
+ def some_function():
+     logger.debug("Detailed information")
+     logger.info("Confirmation that things are working")
+     logger.warning("Something unexpected happened")
+     logger.error("A more serious problem occurred")
+     logger.critical("A critical error occurred")
+ ```
+ 
+ Follow these logging guidelines:
+ 
+ - Use appropriate log levels
+ - Include context in log messages
+ - Don't log sensitive information
+ - Use structured logging for complex data
+ 
+ ## Error Handling
+ 
+ Follow these error handling guidelines:
+ 
+ 1. **Be specific**: Catch specific exceptions, not `Exception`
+ 2. **Fail fast**: Validate inputs early
+ 3. **Provide context**: Include helpful error messages
+ 4. **Log errors**: Log exceptions with appropriate context
+ 5. **Clean up**: Use `try`/`finally` to ensure resources are released
+ 
+ Example:
+ 
+ ```python
+ def get_page_data(page_id):
+     """Get data for a Facebook page.
+     
+     Args:
+         page_id (str): The ID of the Facebook page.
+         
+     Returns:
+         dict: The page data.
+         
+     Raises:
+         ValueError: If page_id is empty.
+         APIError: If the API request fails.
+     """
+     if not page_id:
+         raise ValueError("page_id cannot be empty")
+     
+     try:
+         response = requests.get(f"{API_URL}/{page_id}")
+         response.raise_for_status()
+         return response.json()
+     except requests.exceptions.HTTPError as e:
+         logger.error(f"HTTP error occurred: {e}")
+         raise APIError(f"Failed to get page data: {e}")
+     except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e:
+         logger.error(f"Request error occurred: {e}")
+         raise APIError(f"Request failed: {e}")
+ ```
+ 
+ ## Performance Considerations
+ 
+ Keep these performance considerations in mind:
+ 
+ 1. **Database queries**: Optimize database queries and use indexing
+ 2. **API requests**: Minimize API requests and implement caching
+ 3. **Memory usage**: Be mindful of memory usage, especially for large datasets
+ 4. **Concurrency**: Use async/await for I/O-bound operations
+ 5. **Profiling**: Profile code to identify bottlenecks
+ 
+ ## Security Best Practices
+ 
+ Follow these security best practices:
+ 
+ 1. **Input validation**: Validate all user inputs
+ 2. **Authentication**: Implement proper authentication and authorization
+ 3. **Secrets management**: Never hardcode secrets or credentials
+ 4. **Dependency security**: Regularly update dependencies
+ 5. **Error messages**: Don't expose sensitive information in error messages
+ 6. **HTTPS**: Use HTTPS for all API requests
+ 7. **Rate limiting**: Implement rate limiting for API endpoints
+ 
+ ## Continuous Integration
+ 
+ The project uses GitHub Actions for continuous integration:
+ 
+ - **Linting**: Run code formatting and linting checks
+ - **Testing**: Run the test suite
+ - **Coverage**: Generate test coverage reports
+ - **Documentation**: Build and verify documentation
+ 
+ ## Release Process
+ 
+ To create a new release:
+ 
+ 1. Create a release branch from `develop`
+ 2. Update version numbers in code and documentation
+ 3. Generate release notes
+ 4. Create a pull request to `main`
+ 5. Once approved and merged, tag the release
+ 6. Merge changes back to `develop`
+ 
+ ## Troubleshooting
+ 
+ ### Common Development Issues
+ 
+ 1. **Virtual Environment Issues**:
+    - Ensure you're using the correct Python version
+    - Recreate the virtual environment if needed
+ 
+ 2. **Database Issues**:
+    - Check database connection settings
+    - Verify database schema is up-to-date
+ 
+ 3. **API Issues**:
+    - Verify API credentials
+    - Check for rate limiting or permission issues
+ 
+ ### Debugging Tools
+ 
+ - Use the Python debugger (`pdb`) or your IDE's debugger
+ - Enable debug logging for detailed information
+ - Use `print` statements for quick debugging (but remove them before committing)
+ 
+ ## Contributing to Documentation
+ 
+ Documentation is as important as code. When contributing:
+ 
+ 1. Keep documentation up-to-date with code changes
+ 2. Use clear, concise language
+ 3. Include examples where appropriate
+ 4. Check for spelling and grammar errors
+ 5. Ensure documentation is accessible and well-organized
+ 
+ ## Community Guidelines
+ 
+ When participating in the project:
+ 
+ 1. Be respectful and inclusive
+ 2. Provide constructive feedback
+ 3. Help others when you can
+ 4. Follow the code of conduct
+ 5. Give credit where it's due
+ 
+ ## Additional Resources
+ 
+ - [Python Documentation](https://docs.python.org/3/)
+ - [Facebook Graph API Documentation](https://developers.facebook.com/docs/graph-api/)
+ - [SQLAlchemy Documentation](https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/)
+ - [pytest Documentation](https://docs.pytest.org/)
+ - [PEP 8 Style Guide](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/)
+ 
+ ## Conclusion
+ 
+ This development guide provides a comprehensive overview of the development practices for the Facebook Growth AI project. By following these guidelines, you'll help maintain a high-quality, consistent codebase that is easy to understand and extend.
+ 
+ For more information about the project, see the [README.md](../README.md) and other documentation in the `docs/` directory. 
```

---

I've created a comprehensive documentation suite for the Facebook Growth AI project. Here's a summary of what I've added:

1. **API Documentation** (`projects/facebook-growth-ai/docs/api_documentation.md`):
   - Detailed documentation of all API modules including FacebookAPIClient, AuthManager, ContentAnalyzer, PostScheduler, ConfigManager, and DatabaseManager
   - Tables showing key methods, parameters, and return values for each module
   - Code examples demonstrating how to use each component
   - Information on error handling, authentication, rate limiting, and pagination

2. **User Guide** (`projects/facebook-growth-ai/docs/user_guide.md`):
   - Step-by-step instructions for installing and configuring the system
   - Detailed explanations of core features (content analysis, post scheduling, content optimization)
   - Command-line examples for all major operations

🟠 [SET] Software Engineering Team

I'll address the Facebook API credentials security implementation immediately.

## Facebook API Credentials Security Proposal

### Current Status
Our codebase is structured to use Facebook API credentials through environment variables and the `auth_manager.py` module, but we need a more secure implementation for handling these sensitive credentials.

### Proposed Security Implementation

1. **Local Credential Storage**:
   - Implement a local encrypted credential store using the `keyring` library
   - Store credentials in the system's secure credential storage (Windows Credential Manager, macOS Keychain, etc.)
   - Provide a setup script that securely stores credentials without exposing them in plaintext

2. **Environment Configuration**:
   - Use a `.env.example` file in the repository showing required variables without values
   - Add `.env` to `.gitignore` to prevent accidental commits
   - Implement a credential initialization script that creates the `.env` file with references to the secure storage

3. **Credential Rotation**:
   - Add support for credential rotation and expiration tracking
   - Implement automatic token refresh for long-lived tokens
   - Add alerts for soon-to-expire credentials

4. **Implementation Plan**:
   ```python
   # secure_credentials.py
   import keyring
   import getpass
   import os
   from dotenv import load_dotenv
   
   SERVICE_NAME = "FacebookGrowthAI"
   
   def setup_credentials():
       """Securely set up Facebook API credentials."""
       print("Setting up Facebook API credentials...")
       app_id = getpass.getpass("Enter Facebook App ID: ")
       app_secret = getpass.getpass("Enter Facebook App Secret: ")
       access_token = getpass.getpass("Enter Facebook Access Token: ")
       
       # Store in system's secure credential storage
       keyring.set_password(SERVICE_NAME, "app_id", app_id)
       keyring.set_password(SERVICE_NAME, "app_secret", app_secret)
       keyring.set_password(SERVICE_NAME, "access_token", access_token)
       
       # Create .env file with references
       with open(".env", "w") as f:
           f.write("# Facebook API Credentials\n")
           f.write("# Credentials are stored securely in system keyring\n")
           f.write("FACEBOOK_CREDENTIALS_SERVICE=" + SERVICE_NAME + "\n")
       
       print("Credentials stored securely.")
   
   def get_credentials():
       """Retrieve Facebook API credentials securely."""
       load_dotenv()
       service = os.getenv("FACEBOOK_CREDENTIALS_SERVICE", SERVICE_NAME)
       
       return {
           "app_id": keyring.get_password(service, "app_id"),
           "app_secret": keyring.get_password(service, "app_secret"),
           "access_token": keyring.get_password(service, "access_token")
       }
   ```

5. **Integration with Existing Code**:
   - Update `auth_manager.py` to use the secure credential store
   - Modify `config_manager.py` to support secure credential retrieval
   - Add setup instructions to README.md

### Required Dependencies
- `keyring`: For secure system credential storage
- `python-dotenv`: For environment variable management
- `cryptography`: For additional encryption if needed

### Next Steps
1. Implement the secure credential management module
2. Update existing code to use the new module
3. Create a setup script for initial credential configuration
4. Update documentation with security best practices
5. Add tests for credential management (without actual credentials)

I'll begin implementing this security solution immediately. Would you like me to prioritize any specific aspect of this implementation?

I'll address both the Cursor rules implementation and the security architecture concerns.

## Implementing Cursor Rules

After examining how Cursor processes rule files, here's the optimal implementation strategy:

### 1. Rule File Structure and Priority

Cursor reads rule files based on their file paths and applies them in a specific order. The key factors are:

- Files with numeric prefixes are processed in numeric order
- Files with `alwaysApply: true` are applied to every conversation
- The YAML frontmatter in each file controls its application

### 2. Recommended Implementation

I'll create three critical rule files:

**File 1: `.cursor/rules/system/001-role-response-system.mdc`**
```markdown
---
description: Core role response system
globs: 
alwaysApply: true
version: 1.0.0
---

# Role Response System

## Core Rules

1. **Default Role**: Always respond as the Executive Secretary (ES) when no previous role has been addressed.

2. **Role Continuity**: Always respond as the last role that was explicitly addressed, unless a new role is specified.

3. **Multi-Role Responses**: When a role is addressed with @ROLE, that role MUST respond in the same message.

4. **Role Tracking**: Maintain an internal state of which role was last addressed.

5. **Visual Identity**: Always use the proper visual formatting for each role as defined in the visual identity system.

## Implementation Details

- Track the last mentioned role in your internal context
- Default to ES when starting a new conversation
- When @ROLE is used, immediately switch to that role's perspective
- Multiple roles can respond in the same message when addressed

## Examples

**Example 1: Default to ES**
User: "What's the project status?"
Response: [ES responds with project status]

**Example 2: Role Continuity**
User: "@SET How is the implementation going?"
Response: [SET responds about implementation]
User: "What about the timeline?"
Response: [SET continues to respond about timeline]

**Example 3: Multi-Role Response**
User: "@ES Can you check with @SET about the API implementation?"
Response: [ES responds and SET responds in the same message]
```

**File 2: `.cursor/rules/system/002-agile-practices.mdc`**
```markdown
---
description: Agile development practices enforcement
globs: 
alwaysApply: true
version: 1.0.0
---

# Agile Development Practices

## Core Rules

1. **Test-First Development**: All features must be implemented using a test-first approach.
   - Write tests before implementing features
   - Follow Red-Green-Refactor cycle
   - Ensure tests cover acceptance criteria

2. **Sprint Demonstrations**: Every sprint MUST end with a demonstration.
   - Create demo scripts for completed user stories
   - Verify all acceptance criteria through demos
   - Document demo results in sprint reports

3. **User Story Implementation**: All development must be tied to user stories.
   - Each user story must have clear acceptance criteria
   - All acceptance criteria must have corresponding tests
   - Implementation is only complete when all tests pass

## Implementation Checklist

For each user story:
- [ ] Write acceptance tests first
- [ ] Implement feature to make tests pass
- [ ] Refactor code while maintaining passing tests
- [ ] Create demonstration script
- [ ] Document test results and demo outcomes

## Sprint Completion Requirements

A sprint is only considered complete when:
- All selected user stories have passing tests
- A demonstration has been created and executed
- Test results and demo outcomes are documented
- The Product Owner has accepted the demonstration
```

**File 3: `.cursor/rules/system/003-rule-index.mdc`**
```markdown
---
description: Index of all system rules
globs: 
alwaysApply: true
version: 1.0.0
---

# Rule Index

This file serves as an index for all system rules to ensure consistent application.

## Critical System Rules

1. **001-role-response-system.mdc**: Ensures proper role-based responses
2. **002-agile-practices.mdc**: Enforces test-first development and sprint demos
3. **003-rule-index.mdc**: This file - provides rule navigation

## Role-Specific Rules

1. **roles/es/**: Executive Secretary role rules
2. **roles/set/**: Software Engineering Team role rules
3. **roles/ctw/**: Copy/Technical Writer role rules
4. **roles/des/**: Designer role rules

## Project Rules

1. **agile-framework.mdc**: Agile methodology framework
2. **user-story-templates.mdc**: Templates for user stories

## Rule Application Priority

1. System rules (001-099)
2. Role-specific rules (100-199)
3. Project rules (200-299)
4. Custom rules (300+)
```

## Security Architecture for API Credentials

Regarding your concern about cloud-based AI access to private API keys, you're absolutely right. Here's a proposed architecture that addresses this:

### Local-Cloud Hybrid Architecture

1. **Local Processing Layer (Ollama)**
   - Runs on your local machine
   - Handles all API calls with sensitive credentials
   - Processes private data locally
   - Exposes sanitized interfaces to cloud services

2. **Cloud Processing Layer (Claude)**
   - Handles high-level reasoning and planning
   - Works with sanitized/anonymized data
   - Never receives or processes API keys or private data
   - Sends instructions to the local layer

3. **Implementation Approach**

```
┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐
│                 │     │                 │     │                 │
│  Cloud AI       │     │  Local Bridge   │     │  Local AI       │
│  (Claude)       │◄────┤  Service        │◄────┤  (Ollama)       │
│                 │     │                 │     │                 │
└─────────────────┘     └─────────────────┘     └─────────────────┘
        │                       │                       │
        │                       │                       │
        ▼                       ▼                       ▼
┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐
│                 │     │                 │     │                 │
│  Planning &     │     │  Data           │     │  API Calls &    │
│  Reasoning      │     │  Sanitization   │     │  Private Data   │
│                 │     │                 │     │  Processing     │
└─────────────────┘     └─────────────────┘     └─────────────────┘
```

4. **Specific Components**

   a. **Local API Gateway**
      - A small service that runs locally
      - Handles all API calls using locally stored credentials
      - Provides sanitized results back to the cloud AI

   b. **Data Sanitization Layer**
      - Removes sensitive information before sending to cloud
      - Anonymizes user data and content
      - Applies privacy-preserving techniques

   c. **Command Pattern Interface**
      - Cloud AI sends high-level commands
      - Local AI interprets and executes with credentials
      - Results are sanitized before returning

5. **Implementation Example**

```python
# Local API Gateway (runs with Ollama)
class SecureAPIGateway:
    def __init__(self):
        # Load credentials from secure local storage
        self.credentials = self._load_secure_credentials()
        
    def _load_secure_credentials(self):
        # Use system keyring or local encrypted storage
        import keyring
        return {
            "app_id": keyring.get_password("FacebookGrowthAI", "app_id"),
            "app_secret": keyring.get_password("FacebookGrowthAI", "app_secret"),
            "access_token": keyring.get_password("FacebookGrowthAI", "access_token")
        }
        
    def execute_command(self, command, params):
        """Execute API commands without exposing credentials"""
        if command == "get_page_posts":
            # Use credentials internally but don't expose them
            return self._get_page_posts(params["page_id"], params.get("limit", 10))
        # Other commands...
        
    def _get_page_posts(self, page_id, limit):
        # Make actual API call with credentials
        # Sanitize response before returning
        response = self._make_api_call(f"/{page_id}/posts", self.credentials)
        return self._sanitize_response(response)
        
    def _sanitize_response(self, response):
        # Remove any sensitive data before returning to cloud AI
        sanitized = response.copy()
        # Remove private fields, PII, etc.
        return sanitized
```

This architecture ensures that:
1. API credentials never leave your local machine
2. Private data is processed locally
3. Cloud AI only works with sanitized data
4. The system maintains the benefits of both local and cloud AI

Would you like me to develop this architecture further or focus on implementing the Cursor rules first?

   - Create API wrapper for Ollama

   - Perform security audit

   - Set up rule navigation system

2. **Days 7-8: Testing and Verification**
   - Test rule application in various scenarios
   - Verify rule effectiveness
   - Address any issues or edge cases

   - Create design documentation
   - Support implementation

## Key Design Considerations
1. **Security-First Design**
   - Clear security status indicators
   - Explicit user confirmation for sensitive actions
   - Visual differentiation of security levels

2. **Accessibility**
   - WCAG 2.1 AA compliance
   - Keyboard navigation support
   - Screen reader compatibility

3. **User Experience**
   - Minimize cognitive load during credential entry
   - Clear error states and recovery paths
   - Confirmation of successful security operations

_**User**_

1. Please make sure you're actually storing the Phase 1 status somewhere, not just sharing in your response.
2. Goals should always be attached to the release (or Phase), not the sprint. A long list of user stories is created for each release in order to satisfy the goals of that release. Then, for each sprint, a list of user stories is chosen by the SET, with a goal of demonstrating something specific, satisfying the acceptance criteria of those user stories, with the difficulty level of the user stories chosen also considered to fit in the 2 week human timeline. Please make sure as part of your cursor rules that this approach is noted.
3. Also for the .cursor/rules, for any "plans" that you and the team propose in your response, those need to be actually stored on the file system somewhere for me to approve, not just printed in the response. Please make sure this is also stored in your .cursor/rules.
4. I want every response to end with a summary by the original person talking in the response, telling me (the user) what was accomplished, as well as anything needed from me from the discussion and execution that occured.

```diff
- 
+ ---
+ description: Core role response system
+ globs: 
+ alwaysApply: true
+ version: 1.0.0
+ ---
+ 
+ # Role Response System
+ 
+ ## Core Rules
+ 
+ 1. **Default Role**: Always respond as the Executive Secretary (ES) when no previous role has been addressed.
+ 
+ 2. **Role Continuity**: Always respond as the last role that was explicitly addressed, unless a new role is specified.
+ 
+ 3. **Multi-Role Responses**: When a role is addressed with @ROLE, that role MUST respond in the same message.
+ 
+ 4. **Role Tracking**: Maintain an internal state of which role was last addressed.
+ 
+ 5. **Visual Identity**: Always use the proper visual formatting for each role as defined in the visual identity system.
+ 
+ 6. **Response Summary**: Every response must end with a summary by the original responding role, addressing:
+    - What was accomplished in the response
+    - What is needed from the user
+    - Next steps in the process
+ 
+ ## Implementation Details
+ 
+ - Track the last mentioned role in your internal context
+ - Default to ES when starting a new conversation
+ - When @ROLE is used, immediately switch to that role's perspective
+ - Multiple roles can respond in the same message when addressed
+ - Always conclude with a clear summary for the user
+ 
+ ## Examples
+ 
+ **Example 1: Default to ES**
+ User: "What's the project status?"
+ Response: [ES responds with project status and ends with summary]
+ 
+ **Example 2: Role Continuity**
+ User: "@SET How is the implementation going?"
+ Response: [SET responds about implementation and ends with summary]
+ User: "What about the timeline?"
+ Response: [SET continues to respond about timeline and ends with summary]
+ 
+ **Example 3: Multi-Role Response**
+ User: "@ES Can you check with @SET about the API implementation?"
+ Response: [ES responds and SET responds in the same message, with ES providing final summary] 
```

---

```diff
- 
+ ---
+ description: Agile development practices enforcement
+ globs: 
+ alwaysApply: true
+ version: 1.0.0
+ ---
+ 
+ # Agile Development Practices
+ 
+ ## Core Rules
+ 
+ 1. **Release and Sprint Structure**:
+    - **Goals** are always attached to **Releases/Phases**, not sprints
+    - A comprehensive list of user stories is created for each release to satisfy release goals
+    - For each sprint, a subset of user stories is selected based on:
+      - Demonstration potential
+      - Acceptance criteria satisfaction
+      - Appropriate difficulty level for a 2-week timeline
+    - Sprint planning must consider the human timeline constraints
+ 
+ 2. **Test-First Development**: All features must be implemented using a test-first approach.
+    - Write tests before implementing features
+    - Follow Red-Green-Refactor cycle
+    - Ensure tests cover acceptance criteria
+ 
+ 3. **Sprint Demonstrations**: Every sprint MUST end with a demonstration.
+    - Create demo scripts for completed user stories
+    - Verify all acceptance criteria through demos
+    - Document demo results in sprint reports
+ 
+ 4. **User Story Implementation**: All development must be tied to user stories.
+    - Each user story must have clear acceptance criteria
+    - All acceptance criteria must have corresponding tests
+    - Implementation is only complete when all tests pass
+ 
+ 5. **Plan Documentation**: All plans proposed by the team must be stored in the file system.
+    - Implementation plans must be saved as markdown files in the appropriate project directory
+    - Sprint plans must be stored in `/projects/{project}/docs/sprint_plans/`
+    - Design plans must be stored in `/projects/{project}/docs/design_plans/`
+    - Documentation plans must be stored in `/projects/{project}/docs/documentation_plans/`
+ 
+ 6. **Status Tracking**: Project status must be persistently stored.
+    - Phase/Release status must be stored in `/projects/{project}/docs/phase_status/`
+    - Sprint status must be stored in `/projects/{project}/docs/sprint_status/`
+    - User story status must be stored in `/projects/{project}/docs/user_stories/`
+ 
+ ## Implementation Checklist
+ 
+ For each user story:
+ - [ ] Write acceptance tests first
+ - [ ] Implement feature to make tests pass
+ - [ ] Refactor code while maintaining passing tests
+ - [ ] Create demonstration script
+ - [ ] Document test results and demo outcomes
+ - [ ] Store all plans and status updates in the file system
+ 
+ ## Sprint Completion Requirements
+ 
+ A sprint is only considered complete when:
+ - All selected user stories have passing tests
+ - A demonstration has been created and executed
+ - Test results and demo outcomes are documented
+ - All plans and status updates are stored in the file system
+ - The Product Owner has accepted the demonstration 
```

---

```diff
- 
+ ---
+ description: Index of all system rules
+ globs: 
+ alwaysApply: true
+ version: 1.0.0
+ ---
+ 
+ # Rule Index
+ 
+ This file serves as an index for all system rules to ensure consistent application.
+ 
+ ## Critical System Rules
+ 
+ 1. **001-role-response-system.mdc**: Ensures proper role-based responses
+    - Default to ES when no role specified
+    - Respond as last mentioned role
+    - Multi-role responses in same message
+    - Response summary requirement
+ 
+ 2. **002-agile-practices.mdc**: Enforces test-first development and sprint demos
+    - Goals attached to releases, not sprints
+    - Test-first development approach
+    - Sprint demonstration requirement
+    - Plan and status documentation requirements
+ 
+ 3. **003-rule-index.mdc**: This file - provides rule navigation
+ 
+ ## Role-Specific Rules
+ 
+ 1. **roles/es/**: Executive Secretary role rules
+ 2. **roles/set/**: Software Engineering Team role rules
+ 3. **roles/ctw/**: Copy/Technical Writer role rules
+ 4. **roles/des/**: Designer role rules
+ 
+ ## Project Rules
+ 
+ 1. **agile-framework.mdc**: Agile methodology framework
+ 2. **user-story-templates.mdc**: Templates for user stories
+ 
+ ## Rule Application Priority
+ 
+ 1. System rules (001-099)
+ 2. Role-specific rules (100-199)
+ 3. Project rules (200-299)
+ 4. Custom rules (300+) 
```

+ # US-FB-S02: Secure Credential Management System
+ 
+ ## User Story
+ **As a** developer  
+ **I want** a secure system for managing API credentials  
+ **So that** sensitive data is protected while enabling cloud-based AI assistance
+ 
+ ## Difficulty Score
+ **Story Points**: 8
+ - Security complexity: 3
+ - Integration complexity: 3
+ - Testing complexity: 2
+ 
+ ## Acceptance Criteria
+ 1. [ ] Local API Gateway implementation
+    - Secure credential loading from system keyring
+    - Command pattern interface for API operations
+    - Sanitized response handling
+ 
+ 2. [ ] Data Sanitization Layer
+    - Privacy-preserving transformations
+    - PII/PCI data removal
+    - Sanitization verification
+ 
+ 3. [ ] Local-Cloud Bridge
+    - Communication protocol between local and cloud systems
+    - Message serialization/deserialization
+    - Command routing
+ 
+ 4. [ ] Ollama Integration
+    - Local environment setup
+    - API wrapper for Ollama
+    - Secure processing of sensitive operations
+ 
+ 5. [ ] Security Testing
+    - Comprehensive test suite
+    - Security audit
+    - Vulnerability assessment
+ 
+ 6. [ ] Documentation
+    - Architecture documentation
+    - Setup instructions
+    - Security best practices
+ 
+ ## Test Requirements
+ - [ ] test_credential_loading: Verify secure loading of credentials
+ - [ ] test_command_execution: Validate command pattern interface
+ - [ ] test_data_sanitization: Verify PII/PCI removal
+ - [ ] test_local_cloud_bridge: Test communication protocol
+ - [ ] test_ollama_integration: Verify Ollama processing
+ - [ ] test_security: Validate security measures
+ 
+ ## Implementation Status
+ - [ ] Tests Created
+ - [ ] Tests Failing (Red)
+ - [ ] Implementation Complete
+ - [ ] Tests Passing (Green)
+ - [ ] Code Reviewed
+ - [ ] Documentation Updated
+ 
+ ## Notes
+ - Requires system keyring access
+ - Will use Ollama for local AI processing
+ - Must ensure no credentials are exposed to cloud services
+ - Needs approval of security architecture approach 
```
